epigraph.text:
I have set my rainbow in the clouds, and it will be 
the sign of the covenant between me and the earth.

content.text:
While absolute value equations ask for points at a specific distance, 
absolute value inequalities describe regions—all points within a 
certain distance, or all points beyond a certain distance.
The Two Types of Absolute Value Inequalities
There are two fundamentally different types of absolute value inequalities:

items:
**"Less than" inequalities** ($<$ or $\leq$): These describe 
 points *within* a certain distance of a center point. The solution 
 is an interval (a bounded region).

**"Greater than" inequalities** ($>$ or $\geq$): These 
 describe points *beyond* a certain distance from a center point. 
 The solution is two rays (an unbounded region on both sides).

content.title: Solving Absolute Value Inequalities
content.content:
For $a > 0$:

**"Less than" (within):**
\[
 \abs{u} < a \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad -a < u < a
\]
\[
 \abs{u} \leq a \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad -a \leq u \leq a
\]

**"Greater than" (beyond):**
\[
 \abs{u} > a \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad u < -a \text{ or } u > a
\]
\[
 \abs{u} \geq a \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad u \leq -a \text{ or } u \geq a
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
A helpful memory device: think of "less than" as "betweenness" and 
"greater than" as "outsideness."

content.title: Basic ``less than'' inequality
With a linear expression
Basic ``greater than'' inequality
With a linear expression
With coefficient adjustment
content.solution:
\[
 4 - x < -3 \quad \text{or} \quad 4 - x > 3
\]

**Case 1:** $4 - x < -3$
\[
 -x < -7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x > 7
\]

**Case 2:** $4 - x > 3$
\[
 -x > -1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x < 1
\]

**Solution:** $(-\infty, 1) \cup (7, \infty)$

content.title: Inequality with no solution
content.solution:
Absolute value is always $\geq 0$. It can never be less than $-2$.

**Solution:** No solution ($\emptyset$).

content.title: Inequality true for all reals
content.solution:
Absolute value is always $\geq 0$, which is always $> -3$.

**Solution:** All real numbers, $(-\infty, \infty)$.

content.text: Sometimes we need to bound absolute value between two values.

content.title: Bounding absolute value

content.text:
Absolute value inequalities model **tolerance** in manufacturing, 
**error bounds** in measurement, and many other real-world constraints.

content.title: Manufacturing tolerance
content.problem:
A machine part must have a diameter of 5.00 cm with a tolerance of 
$\pm 0.02$ cm. Express this as an absolute value inequality and find 
the acceptable range.
content.solution:
Let $d$ = actual diameter. The deviation from 5.00 must satisfy:
\[
 \abs{d - 5.00} \leq 0.02
\]

Solving:
\[
 -0.02 \leq d - 5.00 \leq 0.02
\]
\[
 4.98 \leq d \leq 5.02
\]

Acceptable diameters: from **4.98 cm to 5.02 cm**.

content.title: Temperature control
content.problem:
A laboratory requires that the temperature $T$ (in °C) stays within 
2 degrees of 22°C. Write this as an absolute value inequality and 
determine the acceptable range.

content.title: Tolerance in Engineering
content.content:
The concept of manufacturing tolerance became crucial during the 
Industrial Revolution when interchangeable parts required precise 
specifications. Eli Whitney's development of interchangeable parts 
for muskets in 1798 marked a turning point. Today, tolerances in 
precision manufacturing can be as tight as a few nanometers.

marginNotes.text:
Great is our Lord and mighty in power; 
His understanding has no limit.

marginNotes.author: Blaise Pascal
marginNotes.text:
In faith there is enough light for those who 
want to believe and enough shadows to blind those who don't.

marginNotes.author: George Pólya
marginNotes.text:
The first rule of discovery is to have brains 
and good luck. The second rule is to sit tight and wait till you get a 
bright idea.
